---
abstract: |
  This chapter introduces neural networks and develops their application to survival analysis. We begin with feed-forward neural networks for regression — their architecture (layers, activations, response functions), optimization (gradient descent, backpropagation), and the progression from deterministic point prediction to probabilistic distributional regression, where the network outputs the parameters of an assumed conditional distribution rather than a single mean. We then show that adapting neural networks to survival analysis requires no fundamental change to the architecture; it is enough to replace the regression loss with a survival-specific objective. Two strategies are developed — a parametric strategy that lets the network predict the parameters of a time-to-event distribution (illustrated with a Weibull AFT example on the tumor data), and a semi-parametric Cox-based strategy that replaces the linear predictor with a neural network. The chapter then turns to two settings where deep learning has a particular comparative advantage over classical methods — competing risks, where a shared trunk learns one representation from which cause-specific heads branch off, and multi-modal data, where modality-specific encoders (for tabular, image, and omics data) feed a common survival head.
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
{{< include _macros.tex >}}
:::

# Neural Networks {#sec-nnet}

{{< include _wip.qmd >}}

*Neural networks* are a flexible class of models that, with enough hidden units, can approximate any sufficiently smooth function [@Hornik1989; @Hornik1991].
General introductions are given in @LeCun2015, @Goodfellow2016 and @Prince2023.
Beyond the basic feed-forward network, a variety of architectures have been proposed, including *convolutional neural networks* (CNNs) [@LeCun1998; @Krizhevsky2012], *recurrent neural networks* (RNNs) [@Hochreiter1997], and *transformers* [@Vaswani2017].

For the purposes of this chapter, the precise architecture is largely orthogonal to the survival adaptation: all of these models share the same mathematical backbone that consists of compositions of linear maps (@sec-nnet-regression) combined with non-linear *activations* (@sec-nnet-activations), trained by minimizing a differentiable loss with stochastic gradient descent (@sec-nnet-optimization).
The adaptation to survival analysis acts (mostly) on the loss, not on the architecture.
We therefore introduce the basics in terms of the simplest variant, the *feed-forward neural network*.

## Neural Networks for Regression {#sec-nnet-regression}

We introduce neural networks in the simplest supervised-learning setting: predicting a continuous response $y_i \in \Reals$ from a covariate vector $\xx_i \in \Reals^p$, given $n$ training observations $\dtrain = \{(\xx_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^n$.
In general, neural networks can be viewed as computation graphs (usually directed acyclic graphs) that transform the input through a sequence of differentiable operations to produce the output.

@fig-ffnn-arch shows a specific neural network architecture, the *feed-forward neural network* (FFNN). 
The inputs (features) are denoted by $x$. 
Here there are three inputs and one intercept term. 
Arrows indicate operations that are applied to the input to produce the output from left to right. 
The final layer is refered to as *ouput layer*. The layers between input and output layer are called *hidden* layers, and each unit in the hidden layer is referred to as *hidden unit*. 
In each step the inputs are multiplied with scalar weights and summed up, then transformed through a known *activation function* (see @sec-nnet-activations), denoted by $a^{(l)}(\cdot)$. 
Concretely, the $j$th hidden unit of the first layer is given by
$$
h_j^{(1)} = a^{(1)}\!\left(b_j^{(1)} + w_{j,1}^{(1)} x_1 + w_{j,2}^{(1)} x_2\right), \quad j = 1, \ldots, 4,
$$
where the scalars $w_{j,k}^{(1)}$ are called *weights* (coefficients) and $b_j^{(1)}$ are the *biases* (intercepts). Collecting them into a vector and a matrix,
$$
\xx =
\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\mathbf{b}^{(1)} =
\begin{pmatrix} b_1^{(1)} \\ b_2^{(1)} \\ b_3^{(1)} \\ b_4^{(1)} \end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\mathbf{W}^{(1)} =
\begin{pmatrix}
w_{1,1}^{(1)} & w_{1,2}^{(1)} \\
w_{2,1}^{(1)} & w_{2,2}^{(1)} \\
w_{3,1}^{(1)} & w_{3,2}^{(1)} \\
w_{4,1}^{(1)} & w_{4,2}^{(1)}
\end{pmatrix},
$$
the entire first hidden layer can be written compactly as
$$
\hh^{(1)} = a^{(1)}\!\left(\mathbf{b}^{(1)} + \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \xx\right) \in \Reals^4,
$$
with $a^{(1)}$ applied element-wise.


![Schematic of a feed-forward neural network instantiating @eq-nnet-forward with two inputs $x_1, x_2$, two hidden layers of four and three units $h^{(l)}_j$, and a scalar output $y$. Arrows indicate the direction of information flow; the bias nodes (labelled $1$) contribute the additive offsets $\mathbf{b}^{(l)}$ in each layer. The output layer is split into the pre-activation $z$ and the response function $r(\,\cdot\,)$ (yellow box) producing $y = r(z)$.](Figures/neuralnetworks/ffnn-architecture.png){#fig-ffnn-arch fig-alt="A fully connected feed-forward neural network with two input units x_1, x_2, two hidden layers of four and three units labelled h_j^{(l)}, explicit bias nodes labelled 1 below each non-output layer, arrows indicating forward information flow, a pre-activation node z, a yellow response-function box r(·), and a single scalar output y." width=100%}



Putting everything together, the FFNN is defined by the recursion

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hh^{(0)} &= \xx \\
\hh^{(l)} &= a^{(l)}\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{(l)}\hh^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}\right), \quad l = 1, \ldots, L \\
z &= \mathbf{W}^{(L+1)}\hh^{(L)} + \mathbf{b}^{(L+1)} \\
g(\xx \mid \bstheta) &= r(z) = y,
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-nnet-forward}
where $\mathbf{W}^{(l)}$ and $\mathbf{b}^{(l)}$ are the weight matrix and bias vector of layer $l$, $a^{(l)}$ is the layer's activation function applied element-wise, and $\bstheta = \{\mathbf{W}^{(l)}, \mathbf{b}^{(l)}\}_{l=1}^{L+1}$ collects all trainable parameters.
$r(\,\cdot\,)$ is an (optional) *output response function* (@sec-nnet-activations).



### Activations and response functions {#sec-nnet-activations}
Both activation functions and response functions are known, prespecified functions that are fixed before training. 
They can be identical, but serve different purposes.

Hidden activation is a modelling choice.
@tbl-activations lists the most common activation functions used in practice.
The default choice for hidden layers is the *rectified linear unit* (ReLU) [@Nair2010] because of its computational simplicity and favorable gradient properties (avoids vanishing gradients in deep networks). 
Note, however, that each ReLU unit is piecewise linear (with a single "hinge" at $v = 0$), so a ReLU network produces a piecewise-linear function whose number of breakpoints is bounded by the number of hidden units.
A tanh network of the same size produces smoother curves because each unit blends continuously — the effect is clearly visible in the `mcycle` example in @fig-mcycle-ffnn below.

: Common element-wise non-linearities used as hidden activations or output response functions. ReLU is the default hidden activation in modern feedforward networks; sigmoid and softplus are most commonly used as output response functions to enforce a probability in $(0,1)$ or a positive scalar, respectively. Tanh appears as a hidden activation in small networks and inside recurrent architectures (LSTMs/GRUs), and as a response function for bounded outputs. {#tbl-activations}

| Name | Definition | Range | Shape |
|:-----|:-----------|:------|:-----:|
| ReLU | $a(v) = \max(0, v)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | ![](Figures/neuralnetworks/activation-relu.png){height=90px} |
| Sigmoid | $a(v) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-v}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | ![](Figures/neuralnetworks/activation-sigmoid.png){height=90px} |
| Tanh | $a(v) = \frac{e^v - e^{-v}}{e^v + e^{-v}}$ | $(-1, 1)$ | ![](Figures/neuralnetworks/activation-tanh.png){height=90px} |
| Softplus | $a(v) = \log(1 + e^v)$ | $(0, \infty)$ | ![](Figures/neuralnetworks/activation-softplus.png){height=90px} |


The response function controls the range of the output and is usually dictated by the application rather then computational considerations. The identity $r(z) = z$ produces an unconstrained continuous prediction (the default for ordinary regression), while non-trivial choices map the unbounded $z$ into the range required by the prediction target (for example a predicted probability needs to be in $(0,1)$).
$r(\,\cdot\,)$ plays the same role as the response function in a generalized linear model, and its inverse $r^{-1}(\,\cdot\,)$ is the corresponding link function.

Common choices of $r(\,\cdot\,)$ are:

* *sigmoid* for binary classiffication: outcome is a probability in $(0,1)$;
* *softmax* for multi-class classification: outcome is a probability vector over $K$ classes (e.g. for predicting the class of an image), $r(\mathbf{z})_k = e^{z_k}/\sum_{j=1}^{K}e^{z_j}$, where $\mathbf{z} \in \Reals^K$ is the vector of pre-activations;
* *softplus* or *exponential* for non-negative outcomes (e.g. income).

In practice the response function is often used implicitly when evaluating the loss function rather than applied as a separate step before evaluation. 
For example, for a binary outcome $y \in \{0,1\}$ with with response function $r$ being the sigmoid, rather than first computing the probability $\pi = r(z) = P(Y = 1 \mid \xx)$ and then evaluating the Bernoulli negative log-likelihood
$$
-\bigl[y \log \pi + (1-y) \log(1-\pi)\bigr],
$$
one equivalently evaluates
$$
\log(1 + e^z) - y \cdot z
$$
directly using the untransformed output $z$, because the latter form is numerically more stable.

### Optimization {#sec-nnet-optimization}

Equations (@eq-nnet-forward) define a neural network, but in order to fit the model to data, we need to choose the parameters $\bstheta$ to minimize a *loss function* $L(\bstheta)$ so that
$$
\hat{\bstheta} \;=\; \argmin_{\bstheta}\, L(\bstheta).
$$ {#eq-nnet-argmin}
For a linear model this objective has a *closed-form* minimum (the OLS normal equations).
For an FFNN — even a shallow one — it does not, and $\hat{\bstheta}$ has to be found *iteratively*:

1. **Initialize**: start from a random guess $\bstheta^{0}$.
2. **Forward pass**: use the current $\bstheta$ to compute predictions $g(\xx_i; \bstheta)$ and the loss $L(\bstheta)$.
3. **Compute gradients**: determine how each parameter should change to reduce $L$.
4. **Update parameters**: take a step in the direction that decreases $L$.

Steps 2–4 are repeated until the loss is (approximately) minimized.
Two distinct concepts live inside steps 3 and 4:

- *Backpropagation* (step 3) is the *algorithm for computing gradients* $\partial L / \partial \bstheta$ efficiently in a multi-layer network. It applies the chain rule once, walking along the network's computation graph from output back to input.
- *Gradient descent* (step 4) is the *update rule* that uses those gradients to take a step. In its plain form,
$$
\bstheta^{t+1} \;=\; \bstheta^{t} - \alpha\, \left.\frac{\partial L}{\partial \bstheta}\right|_{\bstheta^{t}},
$$ {#eq-nnet-gd}
where $\alpha > 0$ is the *learning rate* controlling the step size, $\bstheta^{t}$ is the current parameter vector, and $\left.\partial L/\partial \bstheta\right|_{\bstheta^{t}}$ is the gradient of the loss evaluated at $\bstheta^{t}$.

Practical optimizers (stochastic gradient descent, Adam [@Kingma2015], ...) refine how the gradient is estimated and how the step size is adapted, but share this same skeleton.



#### Regularization and hyper-parameters {.unnumbered .unlisted}

Neural networks are over-parameterized by design and would overfit small datasets without regularization.
Two regularization strategies are widely used.
*Weight decay* adds a penalty $\lambda\|\bstheta\|_2^2$ to the loss for some $\lambda > 0$, identical to the ridge penalty in the linear case.
*Dropout* [@Srivastava2014] randomly sets a fraction $p_{\text{drop}}$ of activations to zero during each training step, which can be interpreted as implicit ensembling over thinned sub-networks.
*Early stopping* — monitoring a held-out validation loss and terminating training once it stops improving — provides an additional, essentially free, form of regularization.

The principal hyper-parameters of an FFNN are therefore the depth $L$, the width of each layer, the learning rate $\alpha$, the dropout rate $p_{\text{drop}}$, the weight-decay coefficient $\lambda$, and the mini-batch size $|B|$.
They are usually selected by cross-validation or by monitoring a held-out validation set; an exhaustive treatment is given in @Goodfellow2016 and @Bishop2006.

#### Beyond fully-connected networks {.unnumbered .unlisted}

The recursion in @eq-nnet-forward defines fully-connected feed-forward networks, but the same training machinery — differentiable loss, backpropagation, mini-batch SGD — applies to any differentiable architecture.
*Convolutional* networks (CNNs) exploit local spatial structure and are the default choice for images.
*Recurrent* networks (RNNs) and *transformers* process sequences of arbitrary length and are used for longitudinal covariates and text.
*Autoencoders* and their variational extensions learn compact representations of high-dimensional inputs.
For survival analysis these architectures are largely treated as drop-in replacements for the feature extractor $g(\,\cdot\,; \bstheta)$: the survival-specific loss function is applied on top of whichever architecture is most natural for the input data.


### Deterministic and probabilistic regression {#sec-nnet-deterministic-vs-probabilistic}

Recall from @sec-ml-tasks-regr that regression tasks can be *deterministic* — returning a single point prediction $\hat y = g(\xx \mid \bstheta)$ of the conditional mean — or *probabilistic*, returning a full conditional distribution $f(y \mid \xx, \bstheta)$ from which means, quantiles, prediction intervals, and predictive densities can all be read off.
This distinction will be central when we adapt neural networks to survival analysis (@sec-nnet-surv), where the prediction targets are inherently distributional. 

Below, we illustrate the two paradigms using the `mcycle` data [@Silverman1985], which collects data from a helmet placed on a crash-test-dummy,  measuring 133 records of head acceleration (g) at successive time points after a simulated motorcycle impact against a wall.
The response is clearly not a straight-line function of time ($x$): acceleration ($y$) is near zero for ~14 ms, then drops sharply, and then bounces back through several damped oscillations.


#### Deterministic regression {#sec-nnet-deterministic}
For deterministic regression, we use the FFNN from @fig-ffnn-arch and learn the parameters by minimizing the *mean squared error*
$$
\hat{\bstheta} \;=\; \argmin_{\bstheta}\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(y_i - g(\xx_i \mid \bstheta)\bigr)^{2},
$$ {#eq-nnet-mse}
which gives a single curve $\hat y = g(\xx \mid \hat{\bstheta})$ summarising the conditional mean, and nothing else.

@fig-mcycle-ffnn shows the learned function for two versions of this FFNN — using the architecture of @fig-ffnn-arch with a single input, two hidden layers of five and three units, and a single output.
Both networks were trained with the same optimizer (Adam), learning rate, weight decay, and random seed; the *only* difference is the activation function (cf. @sec-nnet-activations).
With $\tanh$ activations (left panel) the fit is a smooth curve that captures the impact dip, the rebound, and the damped tail.
With ReLU activations (right panel) the fit is *piecewise linear*: each ReLU unit contributes at most one "hinge" at the point where it switches between zero and the linear regime, so $5 + 3 = 8$ units can produce at most eight breakpoints.
The resulting corners are clearly visible where $\tanh$ blends smoothly.

![Deterministic FFNN fit to the `mcycle` data. Both panels use the architecture of @fig-ffnn-arch (one input, two hidden layers of five and three units, one output) with identical optimizer settings — only the activation function differs. *Left*: $\tanh$ activations. *Right*: ReLU activations.](Figures/neuralnetworks/mcycle-tanh-vs-relu.png){#fig-mcycle-ffnn fig-alt="Two side-by-side scatterplots of head acceleration against time after impact for the mcycle dataset. Left: smooth tanh FFNN fit. Right: piecewise-linear ReLU FFNN fit with visible hinge points." width=100%}



#### Probabilistic regression {#sec-nnet-probabilistic}

The deterministic fit in @fig-mcycle-ffnn returns a single conditional-mean curve but says nothing about the *distribution* of the response around that curve.
For `mcycle` the spread is visibly heteroscedastic — tight before the impact, wide during the rebound — so a constant-variance prediction interval would be a poor summary and doesn't provide uncertainty quantification regarding the mean estimate.

An alternative is to use *distributional regression*. 
The network outputs not just the conditional mean $\hat\mu(\xx)$ but *all* parameters of an assumed conditional distribution.
For the Normal distribution, this means predicting the conditional mean $\hat\mu(\xx)$ and the conditional scale $\hat\sigma(\xx)$ jointly:
$$
\bigl(\hat{\mu}(\xx_i),\; \log \hat{\sigma}(\xx_i)\bigr) = g(\xx_i \mid \bstheta) \in \Reals^2.
$$
The log-parametrisation ensures $\hat\sigma(\xx_i) > 0$ via an exponential response function.
The training objective is the heteroscedastic Normal negative log-likelihood,
$$
\hat{\bstheta} \;=\; \argmin_{\bstheta}\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[\tfrac{1}{2\hat{\sigma}(\xx_i)^2}\bigl(y_i - \hat{\mu}(\xx_i)\bigr)^2 + \log \hat{\sigma}(\xx_i)\right].
$$ {#eq-nnet-gauss-nll}
The parameters $\bstheta$ now parametrize a whole conditional density $f(y \mid \xx, \bstheta)$ rather than a single conditional mean, so the prediction at a new $\xx^*$ is the full distribution $f(\,\cdot \mid \xx^*, \hat{\bstheta})$, from which means, quantiles, and prediction intervals can all be read off.

@fig-ffnn-distributional illustrates two ways a network can produce the two outputs $\hat\mu(\xx)$ and $\hat\sigma(\xx)$ that parametrize @eq-nnet-gauss-nll.
In variant *(a)*, the hidden layers are shared and only the final layer splits into two pre-activations $z_\mu, z_\sigma$, each mapped through its own response function $r_\mu, r_\sigma$.
Because the hidden representation is learned once and reused for both parameters, this variant is parameter-efficient and acts as a form of regularization — particularly helpful on small datasets.
In variant *(b)*, two entirely separate networks predict $\hat\mu(\xx)$ and $\hat\sigma(\xx)$ independently; all weights are still trained jointly because both outputs feed into the same distributional loss.
Separate networks are more flexible — for instance when the two parameters call for very different architectures (e.g. a convolutional encoder for the mean but a small MLP for the scale) or when the two parameters require different regularization (strong regularization for the scale, weak regularization for the mean).

![Two architectural variants for distributional regression. *(a)* A shared trunk whose final layer splits into two pre-activations $z_\mu, z_\sigma$, each mapped through its own response function to produce $\hat\mu(\xx)$ and $\hat\sigma(\xx)$. *(b)* Two entirely separate networks; all weights are still trained jointly because both outputs parametrize the same assumed distribution $\mathcal{N}(\hat\mu, \hat\sigma^2)$.](Figures/neuralnetworks/ffnn-distributional.png){#fig-ffnn-distributional fig-alt="Two diagrams stacked vertically. Top (a): shared hidden-layer trunk splitting into two output branches for mu and sigma, each passing through a response function, converging into a Normal distribution box. Bottom (b): two separate networks for mu and sigma, each with their own response function, converging into the same Normal distribution box." width=85%}

@fig-distributional-regression shows the result of fitting variant *(a)* of @fig-ffnn-distributional to the `mcycle` data: a shared trunk (with the same architecture as @fig-ffnn-arch) whose final layer branches into two outputs.
The response functions are chosen to match the parameter constraints: $r_\mu$ is the identity (the mean is unconstrained) and $r_\sigma = \exp$ (the scale must be positive).
The mean curve $\hat\mu(x)$ traces the same impact / rebound / damped-tail pattern as the deterministic fit in @fig-mcycle-ffnn, but the 95 % predictive band $\hat\mu(x) \pm 1.96\,\hat\sigma(x)$ is now narrow during the quiet pre-impact phase, widens during the noisy rebound, and contracts again in the tail — exactly the heteroscedasticity that was lost in the deterministic fit.

![Heteroscedastic-Normal fit of @eq-nnet-gauss-nll on the `mcycle` data using variant *(a)* of @fig-ffnn-distributional. The response functions are $r_\mu = \text{identity}$ (unconstrained mean) and $r_\sigma = \exp$ (positive scale). The predicted mean $\hat\mu(x)$ (red line) is overlaid with the 95 % predictive band $\hat\mu(x) \pm 1.96\,\hat\sigma(x)$ (pink shading); blue points are the raw observations.](Figures/neuralnetworks/distributional-regression.png){#fig-distributional-regression fig-alt="Scatterplot of head acceleration against time after impact for the mcycle dataset, with a heteroscedastic feed-forward neural network fit: the mean curve follows the impact-and-rebound shape, and the surrounding predictive band is narrow during the quiet phases and wider during the noisy rebound." width=85%}

Nothing in this construction is specific to the Normal distribution: any parametric family $f(y \mid \bsphi)$ with a differentiable log-density can be plugged in, where $\bsphi$ denotes the vector of distribution parameters — in the Normal case $\bsphi = (\mu, \sigma)^\top$, but more generally $\bsphi$ may contain shape, rate, or any other parameters the chosen family requires.
The network outputs $\bsphi(\xx_i) = g(\xx_i \mid \bstheta)$, with one output head per component of $\bsphi$ and response functions chosen to respect parameter constraints (softplus or exponential for a positive scale, sigmoid for a probability, identity for an unconstrained location, etc.).
The loss is the negative log-likelihood $-\sum_i \log f(y_i \mid \bsphi(\xx_i))$.
This is exactly the construction we will use for the *parametric* neural-network strategy for survival analysis in @sec-nnet-param: replace the Normal with a time-to-event distribution (Weibull, log-normal, ...) and replace the i.i.d. log-likelihood with the censored survival log-likelihood; everything else — architecture, optimizer, gradient computation — is unchanged.



## Neural Networks for Survival Analysis {#sec-nnet-surv}

For time-to-event data, deterministic regression does not work, because we cannot calculate the difference between truth and prediction ($\hat{y}_i - y_i$) for censored observations. 
Neural network adaptation to survivival analysis are therefore (almost) all inherently distributional. 
Compared to the general field, deep learning for survival analysis had been dormant until circa 2018, but quickly gained traction since then [@Wiegrebe2024].. 
Nowadays a new deep learning for survival analysis paper is published almost daily. 
Describing each of the in detail is therefore beyond the scope of this book, however, most of them fall into one of three frameworks covered throughout this book: 

- **Parametric neural networks** (@sec-nnet-param): distributional regression with suitable parametric distributions and minimizing a survival-adjusted negative log-likelihood (@sec-surv-estimation-param).
- **Semi-parametric/Cox** (@sec-nnet-cox): optimize the partial likelihood (@sec-classical-cox) as the training objective via neural networks to obtain a more flexible risk score and use Breslow estimator of the baseline cumulative hazard as before.   
- **Reduction techniqes**: converts a survival task to a more standard regression or classification task (Part IV) and uses standard deep learning.

### Parametric Neural Networks {#sec-nnet-param}

The most direct extension of standard regression neural networks to survival analysis is to assume a parametric distribution for the event time $Y$ and to minimize the corresponding negative log-likelihood as in @sec-nnet-probabilistic.
This is exactly the distributional-regression construction of @eq-nnet-gauss-nll, with the Normal density replaced by a time-to-event density and with the log-likelihood adjusted for censoring and potentially truncation.

Recall from @sec-surv-estimation-param that, for right-censored data, the log-likelihood of a parametric model with feature-dependent distribution parameters $\bsphi(\xx_i)$ is
$$
\ell(\bstheta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[\delta_i \log f\bigl(t_i \mid \bsphi(\xx_i)\bigr) + (1 - \delta_i) \log S\bigl(t_i \mid \bsphi(\xx_i)\bigr)\right],
$$ {#eq-nnet-param-lik}
where the network produces $\bsphi(\xx_i) = g(\xx_i \mid \bstheta)$ as in the distributional-regression setup of @sec-nnet-probabilistic.
For a Weibull distribution, for example, the network outputs two positive scalars, the scale $\lambda(\xx_i) > 0$ and the shape $k(\xx_i) > 0$, with positivity ensured by softplus or exponential response functions.
The training objective is then
$$
\hat{\bstheta} = \argmin_{\bstheta} -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[\delta_i \log f_{\Weib}\bigl(t_i \mid \lambda(\xx_i), k(\xx_i)\bigr) + (1 - \delta_i) \log S_{\Weib}\bigl(t_i \mid \lambda(\xx_i), k(\xx_i)\bigr)\right],
$$
and standard backpropagation applies because $f_{\Weib}$ and $S_{\Weib}$ are differentiable in their parameters.

This approach is very general: any distribution with a closed-form density can be used, including the exponential, log-normal, log-logistic, or Gompertz distributions, as well as mixtures and spline-based distributions that allow flexible hazard shapes.
Once the network is trained, a full predicted survival distribution $\hatS(\cdot \mid \xx^*)$ is available for each new observation $\xx^*$, enabling calibrated probabilistic predictions.
This is identical to the parameteric approaches discussed in @sec-classical, but with a more flexible parameterisation of the distribution parameters through a neural network.

As for foundational models, parametric deep learning requires committing to a distributional assumption.
Misspecification of the distribution can bias predictions, particularly in the tails of the survival function.
However, the gain is a coherent probabilistic output and typically better statistical efficiency than non-parametric alternatives when the assumed distribution is approximately correct.

#### Example: Weibull AFT on the tumor data {.unnumbered .unlisted}

To make the construction concrete, we fit a Weibull AFT in three increasingly flexible parametrisations to the `tumor` data set (@tbl-surv-data-tumor) and contrast the predicted survival curves with the corresponding Kaplan-Meier estimate stratified by `complications`.
All three variants use the same Weibull likelihood and the same Adam optimizer; they differ only in *which* parameters depend on covariates and *how flexibly* the dependence is parametrised:

* **M1** — only the scale depends on the single covariate `complications`; the shape $k$ is a single global parameter shared across all subjects. The network's "scale head" is a linear map from a one-dimensional input, the "shape head" is a global scalar.
* **M2** — both scale and shape depend on `complications`, each through a separate linear head.
* **M3** — both scale and shape depend on *all seven* features in the dataset, each through a one-hidden-layer MLP (16 ReLU units), yielding fully *individualised* predicted distributions.

@fig-weibull-aft-nn shows the predicted survival curves for the three variants. 
It illustrates two points.
First, *which* parameters depend on the features matters: M1 imposes proportional decay (same shape, scaled time), M2 lifts that restriction by letting the shape vary across groups, and M3 lets each individual carry their own $(\lambda_i, k_i)$ pair.
Second, despite the very different output structures of the three variants, the underlying model class — Weibull AFT — and the training procedure are identical; only the architecture of the parameter-emitting heads is changed.
It should be noted that the model was not trained to optimize predictive performance, but to illustrate the flexibility of the neural network approach, so the complex model might not generalize to unseen data.

![Weibull AFT fit to the `tumor` data via a neural network, in three increasingly flexible parametrisations. *Left (M1)*: only $\log\lambda$ depends on `complications`; the shape $k$ is global, so the two predicted curves share the same shape and differ only in scale. *Middle (M2)*: both $\log\lambda$ and $\log k$ depend on `complications`; the predicted curves can now bend differently, and the no-complications curve flattens earlier than in M1. *Right (M3)*: both heads are MLPs over all seven covariates and produce one Weibull distribution *per patient*; the resulting fan of individualised predicted curves is coloured by complications status, with the Kaplan-Meier estimates overlaid for reference.](Figures/neuralnetworks/weibull-aft-nn.png){#fig-weibull-aft-nn fig-alt="Three side-by-side panels showing predicted survival curves vs. Kaplan-Meier estimates stratified by complications. Left and middle panels each show two AFT curves (no complications in blue, complications in red) against dotted KM step curves; the middle panel's curves bend more flexibly. The right panel shows 776 individualised AFT curves coloured by complications group, fanned out around the KM curves." width=100%}



### Semi-Parametric: Cox-based Neural Networks {#sec-nnet-cox}

The Cox proportional hazards model (@sec-classical-cox) avoids the distributional assumption by specifying only the relative hazard through a linear predictor $\eta_i = \xx_i^\trans \bsbeta$.
This semi-parametric structure carries over naturally to neural networks: instead of a linear predictor, a neural network $g(\,\cdot\,; \bstheta): \Reals^p \to \Reals$ outputs a scalar risk score $\eta_i = g(\xx_i; \bstheta)$ and the Cox partial likelihood is used as the training objective.

The negative partial log-likelihood (see @eq-lpartial) is
$$
-\ell^{PH}(\bstheta) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta_i\left[g(\xx_i; \bstheta) - \log\!\left(\sum_{j \in \calR_{t_i}} \exp(g(\xx_j; \bstheta))\right)\right]
$$ {#eq-nnet-cox-loss}
where $\calR_{t_i} = \{j : t_j \geq t_i\}$ is the risk set at time $t_i$.
This loss is differentiable in $\bstheta$ and can be minimized by backpropagation.

To obtain a full predicted survival distribution from the risk score $\hat\eta_i = g(\xx_i \mid \hat{\bstheta})$, the Breslow estimator of the baseline cumulative hazard $\hatH_0$ is combined with $\hat\eta_i$, exactly as in the standard Cox model (@sec-classical-cox).

As for the parameteric approach, the extension of Cox based models to neural networks essentially makes the predictor (risk score) more flexible, but does not change the underlying model class or solve its problems. Model @eq-nnet-cox-loss for example still assumes proportional hazards, even though it allows for non-linear effects and interactions.

Another limitation is that the likelihood @eq-nnet-cox-loss depends on the entire risk set, which does not play well with mini-batching and could lead to high computation cost, especially for more complex multi-modal data with images.
This is addressed by *risk-set sub-sampling*: at each iteration the risk set $\calR_{t_i}$ is replaced by its intersection with the current mini-batch, which yields a stochastic but unbiased gradient estimate for the partial likelihood gradient on average [@Katzman2018; @Kvamme2019a].


## Strengths of Deep Learning for Survival Analysis {#sec-nnet-strengths}

The three strategies above already make neural networks competitive with classical and tree-based models on tabular survival data, but they do not yet exhibit a clear comparative advantage [@Wiegrebe2024].
Two settings, however, play to neural networks' specific strengths: settings with multiple competing event types, where a *shared representation* can be learned once and re-used by cause-specific heads, and *multi-modal* data, where modality-specific encoders (CNNs for images, MLPs for tabular data, etc.) can be plugged in and trained jointly under a single survival objective.

### Competing Risks via Shared Networks {#sec-nnet-cr}

In the competing-risks setting (@sec-competing-risks) a subject can transition to one of $Q$ mutually exclusive terminal states, with cause-specific hazards $h_q(t \mid \xx)$ for $q = 1,\ldots,Q$.
A naïve neural-network solution is to train $K$ separate single-event networks, one per cause, each ignoring observations whose event is of a different cause (treating them as censored at the time of the competing event).
The richer alternative — and the dominant pattern in the deep-learning–for-survival literature [@Wiegrebe2024] — is to share parameters across causes by stacking a common *trunk* that maps inputs to a shared representation, followed by $Q$ cause-specific *heads* that produce the cause-specific predictions (@fig-shared-trunk-cr).

![Shared-trunk architecture for competing risks. A common feature extractor maps the covariates $\xx$ to a learned representation, which is then routed into $Q$ cause-specific heads producing the cause-specific hazards $\hat{h}_q(t \mid \xx)$ or, equivalently, the cause-specific cumulative incidence functions. The shared trunk encourages parameter sharing across causes — a form of multi-task learning that typically improves sample efficiency when the same covariates are predictive of multiple event types.](Figures/neuralnetworks/shared-trunk-cr.png){#fig-shared-trunk-cr fig-alt="A diagram showing input features routed into a single shared trunk of hidden layers, which then branches into Q parallel cause-specific heads, each producing a cause-specific hazard estimate." width=90%}

Formally, decompose the network as $g(\xx; \bstheta) = (\psi_1(\phi(\xx)), \ldots, \psi_Q(\phi(\xx)))$, where $\phi$ is the shared trunk and $\psi_q$ are the cause-specific heads.
Training is by joint minimization of the sum of cause-specific losses, e.g.
$$
-\ell_{\text{CR}}(\bstheta \mid \dtrain) = \sum_{q=1}^{Q} \left[-\ell^{(q)}(\bstheta \mid \dtrain)\right],
$$
where $\ell^{(q)}$ is the cause-$q$ contribution under one of the three strategies of @sec-nnet-surv (the cause-specific partial likelihood for a Cox-based head, a discrete-time log-likelihood with softmax-over-causes for a discrete-time head, or a parametric sub-distribution log-likelihood).
For the discrete-time strategy, the head is most naturally parametrized as a $J \times (Q+1)$ output matrix with a softmax over causes (the extra column being the "no event in $j$" probability), as in *DeepHit* [@Lee2018a]; for the Cox-based strategy, one head per cause is trained on the cause-specific partial likelihood; and for the piecewise-exponential strategy, the heads predict cause-specific log-hazards on the shared partition $\mathcal{A}$.

The shared-trunk pattern is an instance of *multi-task learning*: the trunk is encouraged to learn features that are useful for predicting all causes simultaneously, which acts as a form of regularization and typically improves sample efficiency when the same covariates are predictive of multiple event types.
Beyond competing risks, the same pattern extends to *multi-state* models (@sec-multi-state), where one head is added per transition; *recurrent events*, where additional outputs predict the time and type of the next event; and to joint modelling of event time with auxiliary outcomes (e.g. tumour response).

### Multi-modal Data {#sec-nnet-multimodal}

A second comparative advantage of neural networks is their ability to learn jointly from heterogeneous *modalities*: tabular clinical features, medical images, free text, gene-expression vectors, or wearable-device time series.
Each modality has its own natural architectural prior — convolutional networks for images, transformers for text, MLPs for tabular features — but all of them produce real-valued embeddings that can be concatenated (or otherwise fused) and fed into a single survival head.
The whole network is trained end-to-end against a single survival loss, so the modality-specific encoders learn features that are directly informative for the survival outcome rather than for a generic upstream task (@fig-multimodal-fusion).

![Multi-modal survival architecture. Each modality is processed by a dedicated encoder — for example, a multi-layer perceptron for tabular features, a convolutional network for images, and an autoencoder or MLP for omics vectors — whose outputs are concatenated (or fused with an attention mechanism) and passed to a shared survival head. The full network is trained end-to-end against a single survival loss, so each encoder learns representations that are directly informative for the survival outcome.](Figures/neuralnetworks/multimodal-fusion.png){#fig-multimodal-fusion fig-alt="A diagram showing three input modalities — tabular data, images, and omics vectors — fed into three modality-specific encoders. The encoder outputs are concatenated in a fusion block, which feeds into a single shared survival head." width=95%}

Formally, generalize the linear predictor from a single covariate vector $\xx$ to a tuple $(\xx, \mathbf{z}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{z}_G)$ of modalities and write
$$
\eta_i = f(\xx_i) + \sum_{g=1}^{G} d_g(\mathbf{z}_{ig}),
$$ {#eq-multimodal}
where $f$ is the tabular encoder and $d_g$ are the modality-specific embedding networks for $G$ unstructured modalities (image, text, omics, …) [@Wiegrebe2024].
The right-hand side of @eq-multimodal is then fed into any of the three survival loss functions of @sec-nnet-surv.

This composability is the main practical advantage of neural networks over other survival models: while classical and tree-based models can in principle be applied to flattened images or one-hot text, they cannot exploit the local or sequential structure of the raw signal.
Concrete instantiations in the literature include CNNs on histopathology slides combined with structured demographic and molecular features [@Wiegrebe2024], pre-trained ResNets on radiological images with tabular clinical features, and joint modelling of multi-omics inputs through modality-specific autoencoders followed by a Cox loss.


## Conclusion

:::: {.callout-warning icon=false}

## Key takeaways

* Neural networks are adapted to survival analysis by pairing a suitable network architecture with a survival-aware loss function derived from the same likelihoods used in classical and semi-parametric models.
* Three complementary strategies exist: parametric (distributional output), semi-parametric Cox-based (ranking output), and non-parametric discrete- or piecewise-time (direct hazard estimation). The choice depends on modelling assumptions and the type of prediction required.
* All three strategies are compatible with standard deep-learning frameworks and differ from the vanilla regression/classification case only in their loss function.
* Competing risks and multi-modal data are the two settings in which neural networks have a clear comparative advantage: shared-trunk architectures enable parameter sharing across causes, and modality-specific encoders compose freely under a single survival objective.
* The discrete- and piecewise-time strategies are the direct deep-learning analogues of the partition-based reduction techniques (@sec-partition-based-reductions); the neural network can be used as the (multi-output) classification or Poisson-regression model in those reductions.

::::

:::: {.callout-important icon=false}

## Limitations

* Neural networks are data-hungry: unlike classical survival models they tend to require larger sample sizes to achieve good performance, particularly when the event rate is low.
* Training stability is sensitive to hyper-parameter choices (learning rate, architecture, regularization). Careful tuning and multiple random restarts are often necessary.
* Predictions from neural networks are difficult to interpret compared to, e.g., the Cox model, where coefficients have a direct hazard-ratio interpretation.
* The computational cost of the Cox partial likelihood (@eq-nnet-cox-loss) over large risk sets can be prohibitive; mini-batch risk-set sub-sampling introduces noise that may affect convergence.
* Many deep survival methods address only single-risk, right-censored data on a single modality; left-truncation, interval-censoring and recurrent events remain comparatively underdeveloped [@Wiegrebe2024].

::::

:::: {.callout-tip icon=false}

## Further reading

* @Goodfellow2016 and @Bishop2006 for general-purpose treatments of neural networks and deep learning.
* @Wiegrebe2024 for a comprehensive review of deep learning for survival analysis covering architectures, loss functions and benchmark comparisons; the companion table at <https://survival-org.github.io/DL4Survival> indexes over 60 methods along the taxonomy used in this chapter.
* @chen2024Introduction for a self-contained introduction to deep survival analysis, covering classical and modern approaches including competing risks and dynamic settings.
* @Faraggi1995 and @Katzman2018 for the neural-Cox approach (DeepSurv); @Kvamme2019a for Cox-Time and a unified `pycox` implementation [@pkgpycox].
* @Lee2018a for DeepHit and the competing-risks discrete-time approach.
* @Gensheimer2019 for Nnet-survival; @Biganzoli1998 for the earlier PLANN model that pioneered discrete-time neural survival.
* @Kvamme2021 for PC-Hazard; @Kopper2021 for DeepPAMM and the unification with piecewise-exponential additive models.
* @pkgkeras and @pkgtensorflow for general deep-learning frameworks; @pkgsksurvival for a classical-style survival library that interoperates with neural-network–based models.

::::
